author: Dogmatic Treatises, Ethical Works, and Sermons
book: On the Duties of the Clergy.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Dogmatic Treatises, Ethical Works, and Sermons.
On the Duties of the Clergy.

Chapter XXVIII. Mercy must be freely shown even though it brings an odium of its own. With regard to this, reference is made to the well-known story about the sacred vessels which were broken up by Ambrose to pay for the redemption of captives; and very beautiful advice is given about the right use of the gold and silver which the Church possesses. Next, after showing from the action of holy Lawrence what are the true treasures of the Church, certain rules are laid down which ought to be observed in melting down and employing for such uses the consecrated vessels of the Church.

metadata.author: Dogmatic Treatises, Ethical Works, and Sermons

title: Chapter XXVIII. Mercy must be freely shown even though it brings an odium of its own. With regard to this, reference is made to the well-known story about the sacred vessels which were broken up by Ambrose to pay for the redemption of captives; and very beautiful advice is given about the right use of the gold and silver which the Church possesses. Next, after showing from the action of holy Lawrence what are the true treasures of the Church, certain rules are laid down which ought to be observed in melting down and employing for such uses the consecrated vessels of the Church.

source.title: NPNF2-10. Ambrose: Selected Works and Letters

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is a very great incentive to mercy to share in others’ misfortunes, to help the needs of others as far as our means allow, and sometimes even beyond them.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For it is better for mercy’s sake to take up a case, or to suffer odium rather than to show hard feeling.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: So I once brought odium on myself because I broke up the sacred vessels to redeem captives—a fact that could displease the Arians.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Not that it displeased them as an act, but as being a thing in which they could take hold of something for which to blame me.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Who can be so hard, cruel, iron-hearted, as to be displeased because a man is redeemed from death, or a woman from barbarian impurities, things that are worse than death, or boys and girls and infants from the pollution of idols, whereby through fear of death they were defiled?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Although we did not act thus without good reason, yet we have followed it up among the people so as to confess and to add again and again that it was far better to preserve souls than gold for the Lord.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For He Who sent the apostles without gold also brought together the churches without gold.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: The Church has gold, not to store up, but to lay out, and to spend on those who need.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What necessity is there to guard what is of no good?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Do we not know how much gold and silver the Assyrians took out of the temple of the Lord?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Is it not much better that the priests should melt it down for the sustenance of the poor, if other supplies fail, than that a sacrilegious enemy should carry it off and defile it?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Would not the Lord Himself say: Why didst thou suffer so many needy to die of hunger?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Surely thou hadst gold?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Thou shouldst have given them sustenance.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Why are so many captives brought on the slave market, and why are so many unredeemed left to be slain by the enemy?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It had been better to preserve living vessels than gold ones.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: To this no answer could be given.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For what wouldst thou say: I feared that the temple of God would need its ornaments?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: He would answer: The sacraments need not gold, nor are they proper to gold only—for they are not bought with gold.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: The glory of the sacraments is the redemption of captives.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Truly they are precious vessels, for they redeem men from death.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: That, indeed, is the true treasure of the Lord which effects what His blood effected.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Then, indeed, is the vessel of the Lord’s blood recognized, when one sees in either redemption, so that the chalice redeems from the enemy those whom His blood redeemed from sin.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: How beautifully it is said, when long lines of captives are redeemed by the Church: These Christ has redeemed.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Behold the gold that can be tried, behold the useful gold, behold the gold of Christ which frees from death, behold the gold whereby modesty is redeemed and chastity is preserved.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: These, then, I preferred to hand over to you as free men, rather than to store up the gold.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: This crowd of captives, this company surely is more glorious than the sight of cups.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: The gold of the Redeemer ought to contribute to this work so as to redeem those in danger.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: I recognize the fact that the blood of Christ not only glows in cups of gold, but also by the office of redemption has impressed upon them the power of the divine operation.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Such gold the holy martyr Lawrence preserved for the Lord.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For when the treasures of the Church were demanded from him, he promised that he would show them.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: On the following day he brought the poor together.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: When asked where the treasures were which he had promised, he pointed to the poor, saying: “These are the treasures of the Church.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And truly they were treasures, in whom Christ lives, in whom there is faith in Him.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: So, too, the Apostle says: “We have this treasure in earthen vessels.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What greater treasures has Christ than those in whom He says He Himself lives?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For thus it is written: “I was hungry and ye gave Me to eat, I was thirsty and ye gave Me to drink, I was a stranger and ye took Me in.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And again: “What thou didst to one of these, thou didst it unto Me.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What better treasures has Jesus than those in which He loves to be seen?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: These treasures Lawrence pointed out, and prevailed, for the persecutors could not take them away.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Jehoiachim, who preserved his gold during the siege and spent it not in providing food, saw his gold carried off, and himself led into captivity.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Lawrence, who preferred to spend the gold of the Church on the poor, rather than to keep it in hand for the persecutor, received the sacred crown of martyrdom for the unique and deep-sighted vigour of his meaning.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Or was it perhaps said to holy Lawrence: “Thou shouldst not spend the treasures of the Church, or sell the sacred vessels”?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is necessary that every one should fill this office, with genuine good faith and clear-sighted forethought.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: If any one derives profit from it for himself it is a crime, but if he spends the treasures on the poor, or redeems captives, he shows mercy.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For no one can say: Why does the poor man live?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: None can complain that captives are redeemed, none can find fault because a temple of the Lord is built, none can be angry because a plot of ground has been enlarged for the burial of the bodies of the faithful, none can be vexed because in the tombs of the Christians there is rest for the dead.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: In these three ways it is allowable to break up, melt down, or sell even the sacred vessels of the Church.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is necessary to see that the mystic cup does not go out of the Church, lest the service of the sacred chalice should be turned over to base uses.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Therefore vessels were first sought for in the Church which had not been consecrated to such holy uses.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Then broken up and afterwards melted down, they were given to the poor in small payments, and were also used for the ransom of captives.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But if new vessels fail, or those which never seem to have been used for such a holy purpose, then, as I have already said, I think that all might be put to this use without irreverence.
